reviews.comment: The event records intercourse between Tajomaru and wife while the husband is bound to a tree after being lured and restrained—facts that make 'coercion, unambiguously' a well-supported texture reading. The added detail of Tajomaru 'pleading' afterward is plausible woodcutter-testimony coloring that doesn't contradict any event in view.

This reader-frame claims that 'all four testimonies' center different agency around the intercourse. Only the woodcutter's texture description is visible in this view; the other three branch testimonies are absent. I cannot verify or falsify a cross-branch comparative claim without seeing the compared items.

E_wc_wife_goads is an utterance by the wife addressed to both tajomaru and husband. The motivation description—that she shames both men into fighting—is consistent with the subsequent event E_wc_fight (combat between tajomaru and husband) and with the woodcutter's perspective of deflecting blame onto the wife.

E_wc_fight records tajomaru killing the husband with a sword, but nothing in the event structure prescribes the quality of the combat. Describing it as 'cowardly, clumsy' is a legitimate texture addition from the woodcutter's testimony that deflates both combatants, consistent with the woodcutter's apparent motive of presenting himself as the only honest witness.

The events support every factual claim: the woodcutter did steal the dagger (E_wc_theft), and he is the only participant whose knowledge of the killing comes via observation as a bystander (E_wc_fight effects). The 'declines to adjudicate' framing is a defensible editorial stance, and the description correctly flags the tension between candor and self-interest.

This is a well-formed authorial-uncertainty marker. It identifies a genuinely contested interpretive crux—the woodcutter's reliability—and explicitly invites future revision. It is correctly scoped to :b-woodcutter and attached to the trust-flag it questions.

proposed_description.text: The current framing in D_woodcutter_trust—that self-incrimination reads as candor while the film declines to adjudicate—is defensible and probably the safest encoding choice. However, one tension visible in this view deserves more surface: the canonical event E_body_found (τ_s 30) records the woodcutter 'discovering' the husband's body, yet on :b-woodcutter he already witnessed the killing at τ_s 9 (E_wc_fight). His public act of discovery is itself a deception, which complicates the 'candor' reading—he is candid about the dagger but structurally dishonest about his presence at the crime. The 'declines to adjudicate' stance holds, but the trust-flag could be strengthened by noting this double layer: his confession of theft coexists with his concealment of being an eyewitness, and the encoding already encodes both facts. If a future pass finds stronger evidence that the film privileges his account (e.g., the Rashōmon gate frame narratively endorsing him), the trust-flag is indeed the right place to surface that shift.

proposed_answers.rationale: E_body_found on :canonical and E_wc_fight on :b-woodcutter together show the woodcutter publicly concealing his eyewitness status while privately confessing theft—a structural irony the current trust-flag does not fully surface, which bears directly on whether the 'declines to adjudicate' framing is complete.
